summary:
Market Analysis Update: March 26, 2026

The Canadian bond market continues to experience a pronounced "bear flattening" trend, heavily influenced by the spillover from U.S. Treasury volatility following the March 24th hawkish pivot by the Federal Reserve. Despite February’s domestic CPI cooling to 1.8%, Canadian yields have been unable to decouple from the U.S. narrative of "higher-for-longer," as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East sustain a risk premium on energy prices. As of March 25, the 5-year Government of Canada yield moderated slightly to 3.09% from its March 24 peak of 3.20%, while the 2-year yield settled at 2.89%. This narrow 20-basis-point spread confirms that the market is pricing in persistent restrictive policy, leaving little room for the Bank of Canada to lower its 2.25% policy rate without risking significant currency depreciation against a dominant U.S. dollar.

For our institution, the data reveals a critical inflection point in margin management. After holding our best 5-year fixed mortgage rate at 3.94% throughout the early March surge—which saw our lending margin collapse to a perilous 0.74% on March 24—we implemented a necessary upward adjustment to 3.99% on March 25. This move, combined with the slight retreat in bond yields, successfully restored our margin to 0.90%, though this remains below our historical 1.00% target. The elevated 2-year yield (2.89%) presents a dual challenge: it aggressively drives up the cost of our short-term funding and GICs, while the flattened curve prevents us from passing these costs fully to mortgage consumers without losing market share. To maintain institutional profitability and Net Interest Margin (NIM), we must remain agile; further increases in the 5-year fixed rate will be required if the yield curve does not steepen or if the U.S.-Canada bond link continues to pull domestic yields higher despite our softening inflation data.
